questions.stem: A company wants to follow the principle of least privilege for daily Snowflake administration tasks. Which system role should be used as the primary role for creating and managing database objects, while ensuring ACCOUNTADMIN is reserved for account-level operations only?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN, because it has all privileges and can do everything needed
SYSADMIN, because it is designed to create and manage objects without full account-level access
PUBLIC, because it is automatically granted to all users
SECURITYADMIN, because it can manage all roles and users

questions.explanation: SYSADMIN is correct because it is specifically designed for creating and owning database objects (databases, schemas, tables, warehouses) and should be used for day-to-day object management. ACCOUNTADMIN has all privileges including billing and account management — best practice is to use it sparingly and only for tasks that require it. PUBLIC is granted to every user automatically and is not appropriate for administrative object creation. SECURITYADMIN is focused on role and user management (GRANT/REVOKE privileges, CREATE USER), not object creation.

questions.stem: An organization creates a custom role hierarchy for their Snowflake environment. A new custom role ANALYST_ROLE is created but NOT granted to SYSADMIN. What is the consequence of this omission?

options.text: Users with ANALYST_ROLE cannot log into Snowflake
SYSADMIN cannot manage or drop objects owned by ANALYST_ROLE, and ACCOUNTADMIN is required to administer those objects
ANALYST_ROLE cannot be granted any privileges on tables
The role will automatically be deleted after 30 days

questions.explanation: When a custom role is not granted to SYSADMIN (directly or through a chain), SYSADMIN cannot manage objects owned by that role, because Snowflake's access control requires a role to have the OWNERSHIP privilege (or inherit it) to manage objects. Only ACCOUNTADMIN (which inherits from all roles) or the role itself can then manage those objects. Best practice is to always grant custom roles to SYSADMIN so object management is centralized. There is no login restriction from this omission, no privilege restriction on the role itself, and no automatic deletion.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to set a maximum statement execution time of 2 hours (7200 seconds) for all sessions in the account, except for a specific warehouse used by data engineering pipelines which should have no timeout. Which combination of commands accomplishes this?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 7200; ALTER WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 0;
ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 7200; ALTER WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 0;
ALTER ACCOUNT SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 7200; DROP PARAMETER STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS ON WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh;
CREATE SESSION POLICY account_timeout SESSION_TIMEOUT = 7200; ALTER WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh UNSET SESSION_POLICY;

questions.explanation: ALTER ACCOUNT SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 7200 sets the account-wide default. Setting the same parameter to 0 on the specific warehouse overrides the account-level setting for that warehouse, effectively disabling the timeout for queries running on it. LOCK_TIMEOUT controls how long a query waits for a lock, not overall statement execution time. The DROP PARAMETER syntax is not valid for this purpose. SESSION POLICY controls session idle timeout, not statement execution time.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is creating a password policy to enforce corporate security standards. Select TWO requirements that can be enforced using a Snowflake PASSWORD POLICY object.

options.text: Minimum password length of 14 characters
Password must not match any of the last 10 passwords used
Password expires automatically when the user has not logged in for 90 days
Automatic account lockout after 5 failed login attempts

questions.explanation: PASSWORD POLICY supports minimum length (PASSWORD_MIN_LENGTH) and password history (PASSWORD_HISTORY — prevents reuse of recent passwords). Password expiry based on inactivity (C) is controlled by LOGIN POLICY or DISABLE_SUSPENDED_USER_ON_LOGIN, not PASSWORD POLICY. Account lockout after failed attempts (D) is managed through LOGIN POLICY (MAX_LOGIN_FAILURES), not PASSWORD POLICY.

questions.stem: A company has the following role hierarchy: SYSADMIN -> DATA_OPS_ROLE -> ETL_ROLE. The ETL_ROLE owns a table SALES.PUBLIC.ORDERS. A user with only the DATA_OPS_ROLE active tries to drop the table. What happens?

options.text: The drop succeeds because DATA_OPS_ROLE inherits OWNERSHIP from ETL_ROLE through the hierarchy
The drop fails because privilege inheritance flows upward (from child to parent), so DATA_OPS_ROLE does not inherit ETL_ROLE's OWNERSHIP privilege
The drop succeeds because SYSADMIN has OWNERSHIP over all objects
The drop fails because only ACCOUNTADMIN can drop tables

questions.explanation: In Snowflake's role hierarchy, privileges flow UPWARD — from granted roles to the roles that contain them. Because DATA_OPS_ROLE has ETL_ROLE granted to it, DATA_OPS_ROLE inherits all privileges held by ETL_ROLE, including OWNERSHIP of the ORDERS table. Therefore the drop succeeds. This is a critical concept: a parent role inherits privileges from child roles, not the other way around. SYSADMIN inherits from DATA_OPS_ROLE (and transitively ETL_ROLE) but that is not the relevant factor here. ACCOUNTADMIN is not required to drop tables.

questions.stem: An administrator receives a request to create a new user for an application service account with a 90-day key pair authentication setup and no interactive login capability. Which command correctly creates this user?

options.text: CREATE USER svc_app TYPE = SERVICE RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>' DEFAULT_ROLE = APP_ROLE DISABLED = FALSE;
CREATE USER svc_app PASSWORD = NULL RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>' DEFAULT_ROLE = APP_ROLE MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = FALSE;
CREATE USER svc_app TYPE = SERVICE LOGIN_NAME = NULL RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>';
CREATE USER svc_app NO_PASSWORD RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>' DEFAULT_ROLE = APP_ROLE;

questions.explanation: Setting PASSWORD = NULL disables password-based login for the service account while RSA_PUBLIC_KEY enables key pair authentication. MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = FALSE ensures the account doesn't prompt for a password change. Option A uses TYPE = SERVICE which is valid in newer Snowflake versions but alone doesn't guarantee no interactive login without also handling password. Option C (TYPE = SERVICE with LOGIN_NAME = NULL) is not valid syntax for disabling interactive login. Option D (NO_PASSWORD) is not a valid Snowflake CREATE USER parameter.
questions.reference: User Management: Service Account Best Practices

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to implement a session policy to automatically log out idle users after 30 minutes. Select TWO statements that are true about Snowflake session policies.

options.text: A session policy can be set at the account level using ALTER ACCOUNT SET SESSION_POLICY = policy_name
A session policy set on a specific user overrides the account-level session policy for that user
SESSION_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MINS in a session policy controls both the web UI and programmatic client idle timeouts equally
Session policies can enforce a maximum session duration regardless of activity

questions.explanation: A session policy can be applied at the account level (ALTER ACCOUNT SET SESSION_POLICY) and at the user level (ALTER USER SET SESSION_POLICY). User-level policies take precedence over account-level policies for that specific user. SESSION_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MINS applies to web UI sessions; programmatic clients (JDBC, ODBC, connectors) have separate timeout behavior and are not identically governed by this parameter alone. Snowflake session policies control idle timeout, not absolute maximum session duration.

questions.stem: Which system role is specifically designed to create and manage users and roles, but does NOT have the ability to modify account-level parameters or manage billing?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
SYSADMIN
SECURITYADMIN
USERADMIN

questions.explanation: USERADMIN is specifically scoped to creating and managing users and roles (CREATE USER, ALTER USER, CREATE ROLE). It does not have account-level parameter access or billing visibility. SECURITYADMIN can manage all aspects of access control including granting/revoking any privilege (it inherits USERADMIN). SYSADMIN creates and manages database objects. ACCOUNTADMIN has all privileges including billing and account parameters.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to be notified when credit consumption on a specific warehouse reaches 75% of its monthly quota, and have all running queries cancelled immediately when it reaches 100%. Which resource monitor trigger configuration achieves this?

options.text: TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND
TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY
TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO SUSPEND ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY
TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO ALERT ON 100 PERCENT DO CANCEL_ALL

questions.explanation: NOTIFY sends an email alert without affecting warehouse operation. SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY cancels all currently running queries on the warehouse and prevents new ones from starting. SUSPEND (used in option A) would let running queries finish before suspending — it does not immediately cancel them. Option C skips the notification phase. ALERT and CANCEL_ALL are not valid resource monitor trigger actions.

questions.stem: An organization's ACCOUNTADMIN notices that total Snowflake credit consumption is unexpectedly high this month. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides a breakdown of credit consumption by service type (compute, serverless, cloud services) on a daily basis?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY

questions.explanation: METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides daily aggregated credit consumption broken down by service type (virtual warehouses, cloud services, serverless features like Snowpipe and automatic clustering). WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY is at the warehouse level by hour and does not include serverless credits. STORAGE_USAGE tracks bytes stored, not credits. METERING_HISTORY provides hourly warehouse-level data similar to WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator creates a resource monitor at the account level and assigns it to two specific warehouses. Select TWO true statements about this configuration.

options.text: Credits consumed by both warehouses count toward the same shared quota on the account-level resource monitor
Credits consumed by serverless features (e.g., Snowpipe) also count toward the resource monitor quota
A warehouse-level resource monitor on one of those warehouses takes precedence over the account-level resource monitor for that warehouse
The resource monitor quota resets at the start of each calendar month by default

questions.explanation: When a resource monitor is assigned to a specific warehouse, it takes precedence over the account-level resource monitor for that warehouse. The account-level monitor then only applies to warehouses without their own assigned monitor. Resource monitor quotas reset monthly by default (FREQUENCY = MONTHLY). Credits from warehouses assigned to a warehouse-level monitor do not count against the account-level monitor. Serverless feature credits (Snowpipe, automatic clustering) are NOT tracked by resource monitors — they require the BUDGET feature for granular control.

questions.stem: A company uses automatic clustering on several large tables. The finance team reports unexpectedly high credit costs but no corresponding large warehouse usage. Where should the administrator look to identify the source of these serverless credits?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY filtered by warehouse type = 'STANDARD'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY filtered by SERVICE_TYPE = 'AUTO_CLUSTERING'
SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and check the USED_CREDITS column

questions.explanation: METERING_DAILY_HISTORY includes a SERVICE_TYPE column that distinguishes between WAREHOUSE_METERING (virtual warehouse credits), AUTO_CLUSTERING (automatic clustering serverless credits), SNOWPIPE (Snowpipe serverless credits), and others. Filtering by SERVICE_TYPE = 'AUTO_CLUSTERING' will isolate those costs. AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY (option B) also exists and provides table-level detail, but METERING_DAILY_HISTORY is the best cross-service view for identifying the source. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY does not capture serverless credits. SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS does not track serverless credits.
questions.reference: Account Usage: METERING_DAILY_HISTORY, Automatic Clustering Credits

questions.stem: A resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 500 and FREQUENCY = WEEKLY is assigned to a warehouse. On Wednesday, the warehouse has consumed 480 credits. The trigger is set to SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY at 100%. A long-running query is executing when the 500-credit threshold is hit. What happens to that query?

options.text: The query completes normally because it was already running before the threshold was hit
The query is immediately cancelled and the warehouse is suspended
The query is queued and will run when the weekly quota resets
The query runs for an additional grace period of 1 hour before being cancelled

questions.explanation: SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY is the most aggressive trigger action — it cancels ALL queries currently running on the warehouse and immediately suspends it, regardless of whether they were in progress when the threshold was crossed. This is in contrast to SUSPEND, which allows running queries to complete but prevents new ones from starting. There is no grace period. Queries are not queued for the next period; they must be re-submitted manually after the warehouse is resumed.

questions.stem: A data analyst complains they cannot query a table in the SALES schema even though they have SELECT privileges on the table. After investigation, the administrator finds the analyst's role has no USAGE privilege on the SALES schema. Which statement fixes this without granting unnecessary additional privileges?

options.text: GRANT ALL ON SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE SALES_DB TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;

questions.explanation: To access objects within a schema, a role must have USAGE privilege on both the database and the schema. The question states the analyst lacks USAGE on the SALES schema. GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA is the targeted fix. GRANT ALL (option A) is excessive and violates least privilege. GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE (option C) addresses a different missing privilege — necessary but not sufficient on its own, and the problem identified is the schema-level privilege. GRANT SELECT on tables (option D) is already present per the scenario.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator creates a schema using CREATE SCHEMA analytics WITH MANAGED ACCESS. A table REPORTS is created in this schema. Which role can grant SELECT on REPORTS to other roles?

options.text: The role that owns the REPORTS table (the object owner)
Any role with USAGE on the analytics schema
Only the schema owner or SECURITYADMIN (which owns the schema)
Any role with the MANAGE GRANTS privilege

questions.explanation: Managed access schemas centralize privilege management. In a managed access schema, the object owner CANNOT grant privileges on their own objects to other roles — this ability is removed. Only the schema owner (or a role with the MANAGE GRANTS privilege, which is effectively SECURITYADMIN or ACCOUNTADMIN) can grant privileges on objects within a managed access schema. This prevents object owners from unilaterally sharing their objects. Option A (object owner) is explicitly incorrect in managed access schemas. Option B (any USAGE holder) is also incorrect. Option D is partially true but incomplete — the schema owner is the typical grantor.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to ensure that any new table created in the REPORTING schema automatically gets SELECT granted to the BI_ROLE. Select TWO commands that together accomplish this for both existing and future tables.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE;
GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE;
ALTER SCHEMA REPORTING SET DEFAULT_GRANTS = SELECT TO ROLE BI_ROLE;
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE WITH GRANT OPTION;

questions.explanation: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA covers existing tables at the moment of execution. GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA sets up automatic grants for any tables created in that schema going forward. Together they provide complete coverage. ALTER SCHEMA SET DEFAULT_GRANTS is not valid Snowflake syntax. GRANT USAGE WITH GRANT OPTION (option D) allows BI_ROLE to grant USAGE to others, which is unrelated to SELECT on tables and does not auto-grant SELECT.
questions.reference: Future Grants: GRANT ON FUTURE TABLES

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to transfer ownership of a table from ROLE_A to ROLE_B, but wants to revoke all existing grants that ROLE_A made on that table. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B COPY CURRENT GRANTS;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS;
ALTER TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TRANSFER OWNERSHIP TO ROLE_B REVOKE ALL GRANTS;

questions.explanation: GRANT OWNERSHIP ... REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS transfers ownership to ROLE_B and simultaneously revokes all access privileges that were previously granted on the table. This is the correct syntax for a clean ownership transfer. COPY CURRENT GRANTS (option B) preserves all existing privileges. Option A (without REVOKE or COPY) defaults to revoking current grants in most contexts but REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS is the explicit, unambiguous form. Option D uses invalid ALTER TABLE syntax.
questions.reference: Ownership Transfer: GRANT OWNERSHIP REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS

questions.stem:
A security team requires that users from the SALES_TEAM role can only see rows in the CUSTOMERS table where the REGION column matches their own region assignment, stored in a mapping table. A row access policy is created as follows:

CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY region_policy AS (region_col VARCHAR) RETURNS BOOLEAN ->
  EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM region_mapping WHERE user_region = region_col AND username = CURRENT_USER());

The policy is applied. A user with ACCOUNTADMIN role queries CUSTOMERS. What rows does ACCOUNTADMIN see?

options.text: All rows, because row access policies do not apply to ACCOUNTADMIN
Only rows where the user's name appears in region_mapping, same as any other user
No rows, because ACCOUNTADMIN is not in the region_mapping table
All rows, because ACCOUNTADMIN automatically bypasses all security policies

questions.explanation: Row access policies in Snowflake apply to ALL roles, including ACCOUNTADMIN. The policy evaluates based on CURRENT_USER() against the region_mapping table. If ACCOUNTADMIN's username is not in the mapping table, the EXISTS subquery returns false and ACCOUNTADMIN sees zero rows. This is a critical distinction: unlike some systems, Snowflake row access policies do NOT have a special bypass for ACCOUNTADMIN. To give ACCOUNTADMIN full access, the policy logic must explicitly include a check like: current_role() = 'ACCOUNTADMIN' OR EXISTS(...). Option C correctly identifies no rows would be visible but misattributes the reason.
questions.reference: Row Access Policies: Behavior with System Roles

questions.stem: A company wants to restrict Snowflake access to only their corporate IP range (192.168.10.0/24) and explicitly block a known suspicious IP (203.0.113.50). Which command creates a network policy that achieves this?

options.text: CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('192.168.10.0/24') BLOCKED_IP_LIST = ('203.0.113.50');
CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy WHITELIST = ('192.168.10.0/24') BLACKLIST = ('203.0.113.50');
CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy ALLOW = ('192.168.10.0/24') DENY = ('203.0.113.50');
CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy IP_ALLOWLIST ('192.168.10.0/24') IP_BLOCKLIST ('203.0.113.50');

questions.explanation: The correct Snowflake syntax uses ALLOWED_IP_LIST and BLOCKED_IP_LIST parameters. BLOCKED_IP_LIST takes precedence over ALLOWED_IP_LIST, meaning any IP in the blocked list is denied even if it also appears in the allowed list. Options B, C, and D use incorrect parameter names — Snowflake does not use WHITELIST/BLACKLIST, ALLOW/DENY, or the unprefixed forms without the equals sign format.

questions.stem: A company has set a network policy at the account level restricting access to a corporate VPN IP range. An individual developer needs access from a personal IP that is outside the corporate range for an emergency. How can an administrator grant this without changing the account-level policy?

options.text: Temporarily disable the account-level network policy using ALTER ACCOUNT UNSET NETWORK_POLICY
Create a separate network policy that includes the developer's IP and assign it to that specific user with ALTER USER SET NETWORK_POLICY
Add the developer's IP to the account-level ALLOWED_IP_LIST temporarily
Network policies cannot be scoped to individual users; the account-level policy must be modified

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports network policies at both the account level and the user level. A user-level network policy overrides the account-level policy for that specific user. The administrator can create a new policy with the developer's IP included and assign it using ALTER USER developer_name SET NETWORK_POLICY = dev_policy. This is the correct and least-privilege approach. Disabling the account policy (A) removes protection for all users. Modifying the account policy (C) affects all users. Option D is factually incorrect.

questions.stem: A company operating on AWS is implementing AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake. Select TWO accurate statements about this configuration.

options.text: After enabling PrivateLink, all clients must use a new account URL that includes 'privatelink' in the hostname
AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake is available on all Snowflake editions including Standard
Traffic between the VPC and Snowflake over PrivateLink traverses the public internet but is encrypted
PrivateLink requires the Snowflake account and the VPC to be in the same AWS region

questions.explanation: When AWS PrivateLink is configured, clients must use the PrivateLink-specific account URL (e.g., account.privatelink.snowflakecomputing.com) — the standard public URL will not route through PrivateLink. PrivateLink is available only on Business Critical (and higher) editions, not Standard (option B is false). The key benefit of PrivateLink is that traffic does NOT traverse the public internet — it stays on the AWS backbone (option C is false). The Snowflake account and the AWS VPC must be in the same AWS region for PrivateLink connectivity (option D is true).

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is rotating key pair authentication credentials for a service account without causing any downtime. The service account currently has RSA_PUBLIC_KEY set. What is the correct rotation procedure?

options.text: ALTER USER svc_acct SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>'; (This replaces the old key immediately)

ALTER USER svc_acct SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 = '<new_key>'; (Update application to use new key, then run: ALTER USER svc_acct UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY; ALTER USER svc_acct SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>'; UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2;)
DROP USER svc_acct; CREATE USER svc_acct RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>';
ALTER USER svc_acct ROTATE RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>';

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports two RSA public key slots (RSA_PUBLIC_KEY and RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2) specifically to enable zero-downtime key rotation. The procedure is: (1) set the new key in RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 while the old key in RSA_PUBLIC_KEY remains active, (2) update the application/service to authenticate using the new key, (3) verify the new key works, then (4) remove the old key. This ensures the service never has a moment where neither key is valid. Option A causes immediate downtime. Option C drops and recreates the user, losing all associated grants. Option D (ROTATE keyword) is not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: A warehouse has AUTO_SUSPEND set to 60 seconds and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. A batch job submits queries every 5 minutes. What is the expected behavior?

options.text: The warehouse stays running continuously because queries arrive before the 60-second idle threshold expires
The warehouse suspends between each batch run and auto-resumes for each query, charging for each resume cycle
AUTO_RESUME only works if a human manually resumes the warehouse first
The warehouse will remain suspended after the first query completes because the batch job ends

questions.explanation: With AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 seconds and queries arriving every 5 minutes, the warehouse will idle for 60 seconds after each batch completes, then suspend. When the next query arrives 5 minutes later (well past the 60-second threshold), AUTO_RESUME = TRUE triggers the warehouse to start up automatically. Each resume incurs a minimum billing charge (at least 60 seconds of compute). The warehouse does NOT stay running continuously because the 5-minute gap exceeds the 60-second auto-suspend timeout. AUTO_RESUME works for any client connection, not just manual resumption.

questions.stem: A data science team runs very large Snowpark ML model training jobs that frequently run out of memory on a standard extra-large warehouse. What warehouse type should the administrator provision to address this?

options.text: A STANDARD warehouse with SCALING_POLICY = ECONOMY to maximize memory across nodes
A SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse, which provides 16x more memory per node than a standard warehouse of the same size
A multi-cluster STANDARD warehouse with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4 to distribute the memory workload
A STANDARD warehouse with QUERY_ACCELERATION enabled to extend memory capacity on demand

questions.explanation: SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouses provide 16x more memory per node compared to standard warehouses of the same size, making them ideal for Snowpark ML training jobs, pandas on Snowflake, and other memory-intensive workloads. SCALING_POLICY = ECONOMY on a standard warehouse controls multi-cluster scaling behavior, not per-node memory. Multi-cluster warehouses scale for concurrency (more simultaneous users/queries), not for per-query memory capacity. Query Acceleration Service offloads parts of queries to serverless compute for large data scans but does not increase warehouse memory.

questions.stem: A DBA wants to understand the clustering efficiency of a large table before enabling automatic clustering. Which TWO commands can provide information about the table's current clustering state?

options.text: SELECT SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('mydb.myschema.orders', '(order_date, customer_id)');
SHOW CLUSTER KEYS IN TABLE mydb.myschema.orders;
SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('mydb.myschema.orders'));
SELECT SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_DEPTH('mydb.myschema.orders', '(order_date, customer_id)');

questions.explanation: SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION returns a JSON object with detailed clustering metrics including average_depth, average_overlap, and partition statistics for the specified columns. SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_DEPTH returns just the average depth value as a scalar, useful for quick checks. Both accept optional column expressions. SHOW CLUSTER KEYS shows what clustering keys are defined on the table but does not provide depth or efficiency metrics. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.CLUSTERING_INFORMATION (option C) is not valid syntax — the function is a system function, not an INFORMATION_SCHEMA table function.

questions.stem: A table has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 set at the table level, but the database has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7. A row is accidentally deleted. Can Time Travel recover this data?

options.text: Yes, because the database-level setting of 7 days overrides the table-level setting of 0
No, because the table-level setting of 0 takes precedence and disables Time Travel for this specific table
Yes, but only within a 1-hour window because Snowflake has a minimum retention of 1 hour
No, but Fail-Safe can recover data for up to 7 days after deletion even with retention set to 0

questions.explanation: In Snowflake, the most specific (lowest) level setting takes precedence. A table-level DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 explicitly disables Time Travel for that table, overriding the database-level setting. Once data is deleted or modified, with retention = 0 there is no Time Travel history to query. Snowflake does NOT have a minimum 1-hour retention override. Fail-Safe provides 7 days of additional recovery for Enterprise edition, but it is accessible only by Snowflake support, not the customer — and importantly, Fail-Safe only applies if there was retention data to begin with; a table with 0-day retention loses its Fail-Safe coverage as well per Snowflake documentation.
questions.reference: Time Travel: Table-Level vs Database-Level Retention

questions.stem: A compliance team needs to audit which users accessed a specific table containing PII data over the past 6 months. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides column-level access tracking with sufficient historical retention?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY

questions.explanation: ACCESS_HISTORY provides table- and column-level access records showing which users accessed which objects (including reads and writes), making it ideal for PII access auditing. It retains data for 1 year. QUERY_HISTORY shows query text and metadata but does not provide structured column-level access lineage. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY only retains 7 days of data, insufficient for a 6-month audit. LOGIN_HISTORY tracks authentication events, not data access.

questions.stem: A security audit requires a list of all active masking policies and the tables/columns they are currently applied to. Which approach provides this information most directly?

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES filtered by POLICY_KIND = 'MASKING_POLICY'
Run SHOW MASKING POLICIES and manually cross-reference with SHOW COLUMNS for each table
Query SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.POLICY_REFERENCES() table function per schema
Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY and filter for masking policy events

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES is a view that shows all policy associations across the account — including which masking policies are applied to which table columns, and which row access policies are applied to which tables. Filtering by POLICY_KIND = 'MASKING_POLICY' gives a direct, account-wide list. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.POLICY_REFERENCES() (option C) is a table function that works per-database and has 7-day retention, but ACCOUNT_USAGE is more comprehensive for a full audit. SHOW MASKING POLICIES lists policies but not their associations. ACCESS_HISTORY does not contain policy association metadata.

questions.stem: An administrator uses SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and sees a monitor with STATUS = 'SUSPENDED'. What does this indicate?

options.text: The resource monitor itself has been disabled by an administrator and is not tracking credits
One or more warehouses assigned to this monitor have been suspended due to the monitor's trigger action
The quota period has ended and the monitor is waiting for the next reset cycle
The monitor has detected a billing anomaly and suspended itself for safety

questions.explanation: In SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS, a STATUS of SUSPENDED means that the resource monitor triggered a SUSPEND or SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY action against the assigned warehouses — i.e., one or more warehouses were suspended because a credit threshold was reached. The resource monitor itself continues to function and track credits; it is the warehouses that are suspended. This is the expected outcome when a SUSPEND trigger fires. The monitor does not disable itself, it does not self-suspend due to anomalies, and end-of-period reset shows as the monitor resetting its counter, not a SUSPENDED status.
questions.reference: Resource Monitors: STATUS in SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS

questions.stem: A user reports they cannot see data in a view that they previously had access to. The administrator runs SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE user_role and confirms the role has SELECT on the view. Select TWO additional checks the administrator should perform to diagnose the issue.

options.text: Verify the user's role has USAGE privilege on the database and schema containing the view
Check whether the view's underlying base tables have a row access policy that now excludes the user
Confirm the user has not exceeded their session credit quota
Run GRANT SELECT ON VIEW to refresh the privilege cache

questions.explanation: Two common causes of losing view access beyond the SELECT privilege itself: (1) missing USAGE on the database or schema — if these were recently reorganized or the USAGE grant was revoked, the user can no longer navigate to the object; and (2) a row access policy on the base tables may have changed, causing the view to return no rows (which looks like an access issue). There is no session credit quota that affects query execution. Re-granting SELECT does not refresh a privilege cache — Snowflake evaluates privileges in real time and the grant is not needed if it already exists.

questions.stem: An administrator runs ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 3600. Which behavior does this parameter control?

options.text: It limits the total execution time of any SQL statement to 3600 seconds
It sets how long (in seconds) a query will wait to acquire a table lock before returning an error
It defines the session idle timeout before a user is automatically logged out
It controls how long a suspended warehouse will remain in the suspended state

questions.explanation: LOCK_TIMEOUT specifies the maximum number of seconds a SQL statement will wait to acquire a lock on a resource (such as a table row lock or DML lock). If a lock cannot be obtained within this duration, the statement returns an error. This is distinct from STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS, which limits total statement execution time. It does not control session idle behavior or warehouse suspension duration.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to inspect the current value and scope of the LOCK_TIMEOUT parameter at the account level. Which command provides this?

options.text: DESCRIBE PARAMETER LOCK_TIMEOUT;
SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'LOCK%' IN ACCOUNT;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCOUNT_PARAMETERS WHERE NAME = 'LOCK_TIMEOUT';
SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS WHERE NAME = 'LOCK_TIMEOUT';

questions.explanation: SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'LOCK%' IN ACCOUNT lists all account-level parameters matching the pattern, including LOCK_TIMEOUT. The output shows the current value, the default, the level at which it was set (account, session, etc.), and its type. DESCRIBE PARAMETER is not valid Snowflake syntax. ACCOUNT_USAGE does not have an ACCOUNT_PARAMETERS view. SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS with a WHERE clause is not standard syntax — the LIKE filter is used instead.

questions.stem: A user's account has been locked due to too many failed login attempts. The administrator needs to immediately unlock the account. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: ALTER USER locked_user SET DISABLED = FALSE;
ALTER USER locked_user SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0;
GRANT LOGIN TO USER locked_user;
ALTER USER locked_user RESET LOCKOUT;

questions.explanation: When a user is locked out due to failed login attempts, ALTER USER ... SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0 immediately unlocks the account by setting the remaining lockout time to zero minutes. DISABLED = FALSE re-enables a disabled user account but does not clear a lockout state caused by failed attempts. GRANT LOGIN and ALTER USER RESET LOCKOUT are not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is creating a new user for a business analyst. The analyst should automatically connect to the ANALYTICS_WH warehouse and use the ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC schema by default. Select TWO user properties that configure this login experience.

options.text: DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE = 'ANALYTICS_WH'
DEFAULT_NAMESPACE = 'ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC'
AUTO_CONNECT_WAREHOUSE = 'ANALYTICS_WH'
DEFAULT_SCHEMA = 'ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC'

questions.explanation: DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE sets the warehouse that is automatically activated when the user logs in, eliminating the need to run USE WAREHOUSE. DEFAULT_NAMESPACE specifies the default database and schema (in the format DB.SCHEMA) so the user does not need to run USE DATABASE or USE SCHEMA on login. AUTO_CONNECT_WAREHOUSE and DEFAULT_SCHEMA are not valid Snowflake user properties — the correct property for schema defaulting is DEFAULT_NAMESPACE.

questions.stem: A governance team wants to add a description to custom roles to document their purpose. Which command adds a comment to a role named FINANCE_READ_ROLE?

options.text: ALTER ROLE finance_read_role SET DESCRIPTION = 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
ALTER ROLE finance_read_role SET COMMENT = 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
COMMENT ON ROLE finance_read_role IS 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
UPDATE ROLE finance_read_role COMMENT = 'Read-only access to finance schemas';

questions.explanation: The correct syntax to add documentation to a role is ALTER ROLE role_name SET COMMENT = '...'. The COMMENT property appears when running SHOW ROLES and supports governance and documentation workflows. The DESCRIPTION keyword (option A) is not a valid role property. COMMENT ON ROLE (option C) follows a different SQL dialect not used in Snowflake. UPDATE ROLE (option D) is not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Account Administration: ALTER ROLE SET COMMENT

questions.stem: A stored procedure needs to determine the name of the Snowflake task that is currently executing it in order to log task context to an audit table. Which system function returns this information?

options.text: CURRENT_TASK()
SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME()
TASK_CONTEXT()
SYSTEM$TASK_NAME()

questions.explanation: SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME() is the Snowflake system function that returns the fully-qualified name of the task currently executing a stored procedure or other code. This is useful for logging, auditing, and conditional logic based on which task triggered execution. CURRENT_TASK() and TASK_CONTEXT() are not valid Snowflake functions. SYSTEM$TASK_NAME() is not a standard Snowflake system function.

questions.stem: An administrator runs a script that creates roles as part of infrastructure provisioning. The script may be run multiple times. Which syntax safely creates a role without failing if it already exists?

options.text: CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS provisioned_role;
CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE provisioned_role;
CREATE ROLE provisioned_role OR IGNORE;
UPSERT ROLE provisioned_role;

questions.explanation: CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS is the idempotent syntax — if the role already exists, the command succeeds silently without error and without modifying the existing role. CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE (option B) would drop and recreate the role, losing all existing grants to and from that role, which is destructive in a provisioning scenario. CREATE ROLE ... OR IGNORE and UPSERT ROLE are not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Account Administration: CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS

questions.stem: An administrator needs to find all roles whose names begin with 'ANALYST'. Which command returns this filtered list?

options.text: SELECT ROLE_NAME FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES WHERE ROLE_NAME LIKE 'ANALYST%';
SHOW ROLES LIKE 'ANALYST%';
LIST ROLES WHERE NAME STARTS WITH 'ANALYST';
DESCRIBE ROLES FILTER 'ANALYST%';

questions.explanation: SHOW ROLES LIKE 'pattern' filters the role list using SQL LIKE syntax, where % matches any sequence of characters. SHOW ROLES LIKE 'ANALYST%' returns all roles starting with 'ANALYST'. The ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES view also exists and can be filtered with SQL, but SHOW ROLES LIKE is the direct DDL approach. LIST ROLES and DESCRIBE ROLES FILTER are not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: Snowflake periodically releases behavior change bundles that modify existing functionality. An administrator wants to test a specific bundle called '2024_01' in a development account before enabling it in production. Which approach is correct?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE = '2024_01' IN ACCOUNT dev_account;
SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('2024_01');
ALTER SESSION SET ENABLE_BCR_BUNDLES = '2024_01';
CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TEST BUNDLE = '2024_01';

questions.explanation: SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('bundle_name') is the system function used to enable a specific behavior change bundle in an account. This allows administrators to opt in to bundle changes in a dev/test account first, validate impact, and then enable in production before the bundle becomes mandatory. ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE (option A) is not valid syntax. ALTER SESSION SET ENABLE_BCR_BUNDLES (option C) is not a valid session parameter. CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TEST BUNDLE (option D) is not a Snowflake construct.

questions.stem: An administrator runs SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and sees one monitor showing CREDIT_QUOTA = 1000 and CREDITS_USED = 423. What does this tell the administrator?

options.text: The monitor has 577 credits remaining before any trigger actions fire
The monitor's triggers have already fired because 423 > 100 (the implicit 100% threshold)
The 423 credits represent only serverless costs; warehouse costs are not reflected here
The CREDITS_USED will reset to 0 every 24 hours automatically

questions.explanation: CREDITS_USED / CREDIT_QUOTA = 423 / 1000 = 42.3%, meaning the monitor has consumed 42.3% of its quota. If the first trigger is set at, say, 75%, no trigger has fired yet. There are 577 credits remaining before the quota is exhausted. Triggers fire at configured PERCENT thresholds, not at a fixed value. CREDITS_USED in a resource monitor tracks virtual warehouse credits (not serverless), and resets based on the monitor's FREQUENCY period (monthly, weekly, etc.), not daily.
questions.reference: Resource Monitors: SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS Output

questions.stem: A resource monitor has NOTIFY_USERS = ('ADMIN1', 'FINANCE_MGR') configured. When a trigger threshold is reached, who receives the notification email?

options.text: All ACCOUNTADMIN users in the account, plus ADMIN1 and FINANCE_MGR
Only ADMIN1 and FINANCE_MGR, as specified in the NOTIFY_USERS list
The user who created the resource monitor, plus ADMIN1 and FINANCE_MGR
All users with ACCOUNTADMIN or SYSADMIN roles in the account

questions.explanation: The NOTIFY_USERS list in a resource monitor specifies exactly which Snowflake users receive email notification when a NOTIFY trigger fires. Only the users named in that list receive the email — it does not automatically notify all ACCOUNTADMIN users or the creator of the monitor. Users in NOTIFY_USERS must have a valid email address configured in their Snowflake user profile. If NOTIFY_USERS is empty, all ACCOUNTADMIN users receive notifications by default, but once a list is specified, only those users are notified.

questions.stem: Halfway through the month, a business unit requests an emergency increase to their warehouse's credit quota. The current monitor has CREDIT_QUOTA = 500. The administrator needs to raise it to 750 without resetting the usage counter. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 750;
DROP RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor; CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor WITH CREDIT_QUOTA = 750;
ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor ADD CREDIT_QUOTA = 250;
UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 750;

questions.explanation: ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR ... SET CREDIT_QUOTA = N changes the quota mid-period without resetting the CREDITS_USED counter. This allows an administrator to increase (or decrease) the limit in response to business needs while preserving the current period's usage tracking. Dropping and recreating (option B) would also change the quota but resets the usage counter and loses the history. ADD CREDIT_QUOTA and UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR are not valid Snowflake syntax.
questions.reference: Resource Monitors: ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR SET CREDIT_QUOTA

questions.stem: A team is concerned about query queuing during peak hours on a shared warehouse. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view contains data about queued query wait times and warehouse load?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered by QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME > 0
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY

questions.explanation: WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING provides 5-minute interval snapshots of warehouse load metrics including the number of queries running, queued, and blocked. It is specifically designed to help administrators identify periods of overload and queuing pressure. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY focuses on credit usage, not load metrics. While QUERY_HISTORY does have a QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME column, WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING provides the aggregate warehouse-level load view needed to understand queuing patterns. WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY tracks lifecycle events like starts and suspensions.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to understand how storage consumption has changed month-over-month over the past year. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this historical storage data?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE provides a daily snapshot of total storage consumed by the account, including average bytes used for databases, internal stages, and Snowflake-managed stages. It retains up to 1 year of data and is ideal for month-over-month trend analysis of storage costs. TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS gives per-table breakdown but is a current-state view rather than a historical series. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY covers compute credits, not storage bytes. DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY provides per-database historical data at the database level.

questions.stem: An administrator suspects Snowpipe and serverless task costs are unexpectedly high. Which TWO ACCOUNT_USAGE views should be consulted to identify the source of these serverless charges?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY — for serverless task credit consumption per task
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY — for Snowpipe credit consumption per pipe
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — for all compute credit usage including Snowpipe
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_MONITOR_HISTORY — for serverless resource monitor triggers

questions.explanation: SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY contains credit consumption records for each serverless task execution, allowing drill-down by task name, run time, and credits used. PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY records Snowpipe usage and credits consumed per pipe over time. Together these views enable targeted diagnosis of serverless costs. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY only captures virtual warehouse credits — it does not include Snowpipe or serverless task credits. RESOURCE_MONITOR_HISTORY is not a standard ACCOUNT_USAGE view for this purpose.

questions.stem: An administrator runs: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA sales TO ROLE reporting_role; A new table is created in the SALES schema the following week. Does REPORTING_ROLE automatically have SELECT on the new table?

options.text: Yes, because GRANT ON ALL TABLES applies to all current and future tables in the schema
No, because GRANT ON ALL TABLES is a one-time bulk grant for tables that exist at execution time; future tables require a separate GRANT ON FUTURE TABLES
Yes, but only if the new table is created by the role that owns the schema
No, because schema-level grants are not valid in Snowflake — grants must be made per table

questions.explanation: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA is a bulk point-in-time grant covering tables that exist when the command executes. Tables created after this command are not covered. To also handle future tables, a separate GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA sales TO ROLE reporting_role must be issued. This is a common Snowflake gotcha that causes access gaps when new tables are added to schemas.
questions.reference: Access Control: ALL TABLES vs FUTURE TABLES Grants

questions.stem: An administrator needs to remove SELECT privilege on a specific table T from ROLE_R without affecting any other privileges ROLE_R has on other objects. Which command achieves this targeted revoke?

options.text: REVOKE ALL ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t FROM ROLE role_r;
REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t FROM ROLE role_r;
ALTER ROLE role_r REMOVE PRIVILEGE SELECT ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t;
GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t TO ROLE sysadmin REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS;

questions.explanation: REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t FROM ROLE role_r removes only the SELECT privilege on that specific table from the role. All other grants held by role_r on other objects remain intact. REVOKE ALL removes all privileges on that table (not just SELECT). ALTER ROLE REMOVE PRIVILEGE is not valid Snowflake syntax. GRANT OWNERSHIP REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS transfers ownership and revokes grants — it is not a targeted privilege revoke operation.

questions.stem: A stored procedure is created with EXECUTE AS OWNER. Inside the procedure, the developer uses IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('FINANCE_ROLE') to check if the calling user has a certain role. What does this function return in the caller's rights context?

options.text: It always returns TRUE because the procedure runs as the owner, who has all privileges
It returns TRUE if the role that invoked the stored procedure has FINANCE_ROLE granted to it (directly or through hierarchy)
It returns FALSE because stored procedures cannot inspect the caller's role grants
It returns TRUE only if FINANCE_ROLE is the current primary role of the invoking user

questions.explanation: IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('role_name') is specifically designed for use inside stored procedures (especially those running with EXECUTE AS OWNER) to check whether the calling user's active role hierarchy includes the specified role. It returns TRUE if the invoker's role chain includes FINANCE_ROLE — either as the primary role or through role hierarchy inheritance. This allows owner's rights procedures to make access decisions based on the invoker's actual roles rather than blindly executing with elevated owner privileges.

questions.stem: A security review flags a concern: a role has been granted OWNERSHIP on a production table. Why is OWNERSHIP privilege considered a higher security risk than SELECT or INSERT?

options.text: The OWNERSHIP privilege allows the holder to read all data in the table, bypassing row access policies
The OWNERSHIP privilege allows the holder to re-grant any privilege on the owned object to any role, including itself
OWNERSHIP automatically grants ACCOUNTADMIN-level access to the entire account
OWNERSHIP is permanent and cannot be transferred back to the original owner

questions.explanation: OWNERSHIP privilege grants full control over an object, including the ability to issue GRANT and REVOKE statements on that object — effectively allowing the owner to give any other role any privilege on that object. This privilege escalation risk means a compromised or misused role with OWNERSHIP can effectively share the object with any role in the system. OWNERSHIP does not bypass row access policies (those apply to all roles including owners). OWNERSHIP does not grant ACCOUNTADMIN-level access. OWNERSHIP can be transferred with GRANT OWNERSHIP.
questions.reference: Access Control: OWNERSHIP Privilege Escalation Risk

questions.stem: A Snowflake account has a database DB_SHARED that was shared from another account via a data share. A role CONSUMER_ROLE needs to query objects in this shared database. Which grant is required that differs from a normal local database?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE ON DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;

questions.explanation: For databases that were created from an inbound data share, the privilege model is different from local databases. Instead of USAGE on the database plus individual object grants, you grant IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE to a role. This single grant gives the role access to all objects within the shared database as defined by the share's original privileges. GRANT USAGE on a shared database is not the correct privilege. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES is valid for local tables but not for shared databases. GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE is for cross-database views and is unrelated.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to view all privileges currently granted on a specific table PROD.SALES.ORDERS to understand who has access. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE prod.sales.orders;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES WHERE NAME = 'ORDERS';
DESCRIBE TABLE prod.sales.orders PRIVILEGES;
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES WHERE OBJECT_NAME = 'ORDERS';

questions.explanation: SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE prod.sales.orders lists all privilege grants on that specific table — including which roles have SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and OWNERSHIP — along with who granted each privilege and when. This is the most direct command for auditing access to a specific object. ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES can be filtered by table name but has a latency delay. DESCRIBE TABLE PRIVILEGES is not valid syntax. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES can also provide this information as a queryable view.
questions.reference: Access Control: SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE

questions.stem: A Snowflake Native App developer is building an application that uses Application Roles. Select TWO statements that are true about Application Roles in Snowflake Native Apps.

options.text: Application Roles are defined within a Snowflake Native App and are granted to account roles in the consumer account
Application Roles can be granted directly to users in the consumer account without going through account roles
Application Roles are visible in the consumer account via SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION app_name
Application Roles automatically inherit all privileges of the SYSADMIN role in the consumer account

questions.explanation: Application Roles are a construct specific to Snowflake Native Apps. They are defined by the application provider within the app and represent access tiers (e.g., VIEWER, ADMIN) that consumers can grant to their own account roles. The consumer account grants Application Roles to their account roles (not directly to users), then grants those account roles to users through the standard role hierarchy. SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION app_name lists the available Application Roles for a specific installed app. Application Roles do not automatically inherit SYSADMIN privileges — they have only the privileges the app provider defined.

questions.stem: An administrator has applied a network policy at the account level. A specific user VENDOR_ACCT needs access from an IP address that is not in the account policy's allowed list. What command assigns a user-level network policy to override the account policy for this user?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy FOR USER vendor_acct;
ALTER USER vendor_acct SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy;
GRANT NETWORK POLICY vendor_policy TO USER vendor_acct;
CREATE USER vendor_acct NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy;

questions.explanation: ALTER USER user_name SET NETWORK_POLICY = policy_name assigns a network policy at the user level. A user-level network policy fully replaces the account-level policy for that specific user — so VENDOR_ACCT will be governed by vendor_policy instead of the account policy. The account-level policy continues to apply to all other users. ALTER ACCOUNT with a FOR USER clause is not valid syntax. GRANT NETWORK POLICY is not valid syntax. While CREATE USER can set many parameters, NETWORK_POLICY is typically set post-creation with ALTER USER.
questions.reference: Network Policies: ALTER USER SET NETWORK_POLICY

questions.stem: An administrator wants to see all network policies defined in the account along with their allowed IP lists. Which command provides this?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES;
SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.NETWORK_POLICIES;
DESCRIBE ALL NETWORK POLICIES;
LIST NETWORK POLICIES IN ACCOUNT;

questions.explanation: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES lists all network policies in the account, including the policy name, the ALLOWED_IP_LIST, the BLOCKED_IP_LIST, and which users or the account the policy is assigned to. This is the standard DDL command for viewing policy configurations. ACCOUNT_USAGE does not have a NETWORK_POLICIES view. DESCRIBE ALL NETWORK POLICIES and LIST NETWORK POLICIES are not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is troubleshooting a failure where an external stage backed by an S3 bucket cannot be accessed. Which system function can help diagnose connectivity and permission issues with the external stage?

options.text: SYSTEM$VALIDATE_STORAGE_INTEGRATION('my_s3_integration')
SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('my_s3_stage')
SYSTEM$CHECK_STAGE_ACCESS('@my_s3_stage')
SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGE_VALIDATION('my_s3_stage'));

questions.explanation: SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('stage_name') is the Snowflake system function that validates connectivity between Snowflake and an external stage. It tests whether the IAM role/storage integration has the necessary permissions to read from and list the target bucket location, and returns diagnostic information about any failures. SYSTEM$VALIDATE_STORAGE_INTEGRATION is not a standard Snowflake function. SYSTEM$CHECK_STAGE_ACCESS and the INFORMATION_SCHEMA table function form are not valid Snowflake constructs for this purpose.

questions.stem: A company has enabled AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake and also has a network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('10.0.0.0/8'). A client connects via the PrivateLink endpoint from a VPC with private IP 10.5.0.25. Which statement accurately describes the interaction?

options.text: The connection is blocked because PrivateLink connections bypass the network policy IP check entirely, and only specific PrivateLink-enabled roles can connect
The connection succeeds because 10.5.0.25 is within the 10.0.0.0/8 CIDR range in the network policy
PrivateLink traffic bypasses IP-based network policy checks because traffic does not arrive from a public IP; the network policy is effectively ignored for PrivateLink connections
The connection fails because PrivateLink connections must use a separate AWSVPCEID-type network rule, not an IP-based network policy

questions.explanation: When clients connect via AWS PrivateLink, traffic arrives at Snowflake from the private network without a public IP. Because traditional IP-based network policies evaluate the source IP address, and PrivateLink traffic does not present a routable public IP that matches standard CIDR notation, the IP-based network policy is effectively bypassed for PrivateLink connections. To enforce access controls for PrivateLink traffic, administrators should use AWSVPCEID-type network rules to restrict by VPC endpoint ID rather than IP address.
questions.reference: Network Security: PrivateLink and Network Policy Interaction

questions.stem: A security team wants to allow Snowflake connections only from a specific set of AWS VPC endpoints rather than relying on IP address ranges. Which network rule type should be used?

options.text: TYPE = IPV4 with VPC CIDR ranges
TYPE = AWSVPCEID with the VPC endpoint IDs as the value list
TYPE = HOST with the VPC DNS names
TYPE = VPCENDPOINT with the endpoint service names

questions.explanation: Snowflake's Network Rules support TYPE = AWSVPCEID, which accepts AWS VPC endpoint IDs (in the format vpce-xxxxxxxxx) in the VALUE_LIST. This allows restricting Snowflake access to connections arriving specifically through approved VPC endpoints — a more robust control than IP-based rules for AWS PrivateLink scenarios. TYPE = IPV4 uses IP address ranges. TYPE = HOST is for external function and egress domain rules. TYPE = VPCENDPOINT is not a valid network rule type in Snowflake.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to disable Time Travel on a large temporary staging table to reduce storage costs. The table is in a schema with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7. Which command disables Time Travel specifically for this table?

options.text: ALTER TABLE staging_table SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0;
ALTER SCHEMA staging_schema SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0;
ALTER TABLE staging_table DISABLE TIME_TRAVEL;
DROP TABLE staging_table; CREATE TRANSIENT TABLE staging_table (...);

questions.explanation: ALTER TABLE ... SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 disables Time Travel for that specific table without affecting the schema-level setting. The table-level setting overrides the schema and account-level defaults. DISABLE TIME_TRAVEL is not valid Snowflake syntax. Altering the schema (option B) would affect all tables in the schema, not just this one. Creating a TRANSIENT TABLE (option D) also removes Time Travel (transient tables support only 0 or 1 day retention) but requires dropping and recreating the table, which is disruptive.

questions.stem: A developer creates a TRANSIENT table for intermediate ETL processing. Compared to a permanent table and a TEMPORARY table, what are the key characteristics of a TRANSIENT table?

options.text: A transient table is session-scoped (dropped at session end) and has no Fail-Safe period, like a temporary table
A transient table persists across sessions like a permanent table, supports 0 or 1 day of Time Travel retention, and has no Fail-Safe period
A transient table persists across sessions, supports up to 90 days of Time Travel, and has a 7-day Fail-Safe period
A transient table is dropped automatically after 24 hours and has no Time Travel or Fail-Safe

questions.explanation: Transient tables are a middle ground between permanent and temporary tables. Like permanent tables, they persist across sessions and are visible to other users/sessions. Like temporary tables, they have no Fail-Safe period. Transient tables support 0 or 1 day of Time Travel (not up to 90 days like permanent tables on Enterprise). Temporary tables are session-scoped (dropped when the session ends) and also have no Fail-Safe. The lack of Fail-Safe and limited Time Travel makes transient tables cheaper for non-critical intermediate data.
questions.reference: Table Types: Transient vs Temporary vs Permanent

questions.stem: An administrator wants to check the Time Travel retention setting currently configured on several tables in a schema. Which command displays the retention_time column for tables?

options.text: SHOW TABLES LIKE '%' IN SCHEMA mydb.myschema;
DESCRIBE DATABASE mydb;
SELECT TABLE_NAME, RETENTION_TIME FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MYSCHEMA';
Both A and C are valid approaches

questions.explanation: Both approaches work. SHOW TABLES LIKE '%' IN SCHEMA includes a retention_time column in its output that shows each table's configured DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES also includes a RETENTION_TIME column that can be queried with SQL, enabling filtering and ordering across many tables. The SHOW command is faster for interactive use; the INFORMATION_SCHEMA query is better for programmatic checks or when filtering is needed.

questions.stem: A team has a warehouse running mixed standard and Snowpark workloads. After profiling, they determine the Snowpark jobs need more memory but the standard SQL queries do not. What is the correct approach to address this without over-provisioning for standard queries?

options.text: Increase the warehouse size from XL to 2XL so all queries benefit from the additional memory
Create a separate SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse for Snowpark jobs and keep the existing standard warehouse for SQL queries
Enable QUERY_ACCELERATION on the existing warehouse — it automatically adds memory for Snowpark workloads
Alter the existing warehouse: ALTER WAREHOUSE mixed_wh SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED;

questions.explanation: The correct architectural approach is to route Snowpark jobs to a dedicated SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse (which provides 16x more memory per node) while keeping standard SQL queries on a regular STANDARD warehouse sized appropriately for those queries. This avoids overpaying for memory on workloads that don't need it. Query Acceleration Service is for parallelizing large scans, not for increasing per-node memory for Snowpark. While ALTER WAREHOUSE SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED (option D) is valid syntax in newer Snowflake versions, it would convert the shared warehouse and impact all workloads equally rather than separating them.

questions.stem: An administrator queries ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS and sees a table with ACTIVE_BYTES = 5GB, TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES = 20GB, and FAILSAFE_BYTES = 15GB. Select TWO true statements about this scenario.

options.text: The 20GB of TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES is billed at the same per-TB rate as the 5GB of active data
The 15GB of FAILSAFE_BYTES is recoverable by the customer using UNDROP or Time Travel queries
Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 1 on this table would immediately reduce TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES to near zero
Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on the table will eventually reduce TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES as old historical versions age out

questions.explanation: Time Travel storage is billed at the same per-TB rate as active storage (option A is true). Fail-Safe (option B) is Snowflake-managed recovery and is NOT accessible to customers via UNDROP or Time Travel — only Snowflake Support can access Fail-Safe data. Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS does not immediately purge Time Travel data; Snowflake's background processes will clean up historical versions, but there is a lag (option C is false). Over time, reducing retention will cause old versions to expire and TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES to decrease (option D is true).

questions.stem: A compliance team needs to verify all data that left the Snowflake account through COPY INTO external stages or UNLOAD operations over the past 30 days, for data egress cost and compliance auditing. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this information?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered by QUERY_TYPE = 'COPY'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY filtered by QUERY_TYPE = 'UNLOAD'
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY records all data transfer events including data transferred to external stages (egress), showing the bytes transferred, transfer type, source and destination region, and timestamps. This view is used for both cost analysis (data egress fees) and compliance verification of outbound data movement. QUERY_HISTORY captures query execution metadata but does not provide structured egress tracking. ACCESS_HISTORY tracks which objects were read, not data movement volumes. STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY is not a standard ACCOUNT_USAGE view.

questions.stem: A security team wants to identify all active sessions and which client applications (e.g., JDBC driver version, Python connector) are currently connected to the Snowflake account. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides both historical and current session information including client application details?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY — columns: CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID, CLIENT_APPLICATION_VERSION
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS — columns: SESSION_ID, USER_NAME, CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID, CREATED_ON, CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT
SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — columns: SESSION_ID, USER_NAME, CLIENT_TYPE
SHOW SESSIONS; — displays all currently active sessions with client application metadata

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS contains records for both active and historical sessions, including SESSION_ID, USER_NAME, CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID (the client application type and version), CREATED_ON (session start time), and CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT (platform details). This is the view designed for session auditing and tracking which client tools are accessing Snowflake. LOGIN_HISTORY records login events but not the full session lifecycle. QUERY_HISTORY has CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID but is scoped to individual queries rather than sessions. SHOW SESSIONS is not a standard Snowflake DDL command.

questions.stem: An organization wants to use a resource monitor to control costs at the organizational level across multiple Snowflake accounts. A Snowflake administrator proposes creating one resource monitor and assigning it to all accounts in the organization. Is this possible?

options.text: Yes, resource monitors can be created at the organization level using the ORGADMIN role and assigned to specific accounts
No, resource monitors are account-scoped objects; each account requires its own resource monitor, and there is no organization-level resource monitor
Yes, by creating the monitor in the organization master account, it automatically applies to all child accounts
Yes, but only if all accounts are on the Business Critical edition

questions.explanation: Resource monitors are account-scoped objects in Snowflake. They can be assigned to specific warehouses within an account or to the account as a whole, but they cannot span multiple accounts. Each account in an organization must have its own resource monitors configured independently. For organization-wide spending visibility, Snowflake provides the ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema (accessible to the ORGADMIN role) which shows credit usage across accounts, but ORGADMIN cannot create cross-account resource monitors. Organization-level budget controls require using the Budget feature or external tooling on top of ORGANIZATION_USAGE data.
